fuzzy_prd:
We need a command-line argument parsing utility that allows developers to declare each program input as a typed field, and have all the tedious work of converting, validating, defaulting, and required-checking happen automatically. Right now, our engineering teams are hand-writing verbose, error-prone setup code every time they build a CLI tool, and there is no single object whose fields describe the whole input surface.

The new utility must support scalar types (integers, floats, strings, booleans, file paths), collection types (ordered lists, unique sets, fixed-length tuples with per-position types, and variable-length homogeneous tuples), optional fields that default to a null-like absent value, and choice-restricted fields. Booleans should work both as bare toggle flags and as explicit true/false value flags, controlled by a constructor option. Another constructor option should allow flag names with underscores to be accepted on the command line using dashes instead.

Arguments can also be loaded from configuration files (one or more, later files take precedence over earlier ones, and command-line values override everything). There should be a way to populate the parsed state from a dictionary instead of from the command line. A partial-parse mode must collect unrecognized tokens into a separate list rather than failing. Subcommands must be supported, each with their own argument declarations.

All error conditions should map to a small set of neutral categories: a standard parse failure, a missing file, an unsupported type configuration, and a lifecycle misuse. The output format should follow the same conventions as the existing error-reporting module so the calling system can process results uniformly. Results should be rendered sorted alphabetically by field name, with each collection type rendered in the same deterministic style we already use for set output in our metrics pipeline.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON object with arguments/argv/init/configure/from_dict/parse/show/subparsers) -> stdout typed parser result or usage error lines

trigger_keywords: how are fields ordered in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All resolved fields are rendered sorted alphabetically by field name (ascending lexicographic). Each line is 'name=token\n'. An empty mapping produces no output lines.
Set elements are rendered sorted lexicographically by their string token representation (i.e., sorted(render_value(x) for x in v)). The format is '{t0, t1, ...}' with a single space after each comma.

In explicit-bool mode, truthy values are any prefix of 'true' (case-insensitive) or the string '1'; falsy values are any prefix of 'false' (case-insensitive) or the string '0'. So 't', 'tr', 'tru', 'true', 'T', 'TRUE', '1' all parse to True. 'f', 'fa', 'fal', 'fals', 'false', 'F', 'FALSE', '0' all parse to False. Anything else is a usage error.

Scalar tokens: int:<n>, float:<repr> (Python repr), str:<text>, bool:True or bool:False, none (for None/null), path:<text>. List: [t0, t1, ...]. Tuple: (t0, t1, ...). Set: {t0, t1, ...} with elements sorted lexicographically.

A Union of two or more concrete types that is not simply Optional (i.e., not Union[T, None]) cannot be auto-converted. Declaring such a field without providing an explicit conversion function in configure() raises error=invalid_type_configuration.
Declaring a field with type Tuple[()] (empty tuple) is immediately rejected as error=invalid_type_configuration because it has no arguments and cannot be parsed.

Two lifecycle misuses produce error=invalid_request: (1) calling parse_args more than once on the same parser instance; (2) calling add_argument after the parser has finished initialization (i.e., after __init__ completes). Both raise ValueError internally, which is caught and rendered as error=invalid_request.
Pointing config_files at a path that does not exist on disk raises error=file_not_found. This is distinct from a parse failure; it is a FileNotFoundError caught and rendered as error=file_not_found.

In implicit-bool mode (default), a bool field with default=True gets action='store_false' (flag presence flips it to False); a bool field with default=False or a required bool gets action='store_true' (flag presence sets it to True). The flag takes no value token.

When populating via from_dict, all required arguments (those declared with a type but no default) must be present in the provided dictionary OR already set on the instance. If any required argument is absent, the call fails with error=invalid_request (raises ValueError internally).

Config file arguments are prepended before command-line arguments. Among multiple config files, they are loaded in list order (left to right), so later files have higher precedence over earlier files. Command-line arguments always override all config file arguments.

If a collection-typed argument (List, Set, Tuple) has a non-collection default (e.g., null/None or a bare scalar) and the flag is not supplied on the command line, the default value is returned as-is without any collection conversion. So Set[str] with default=null renders as 'none', and Set[int] with default=3.7 renders as 'float:3.7'.

In known_only mode, unrecognized tokens are collected into extra_args as a list of str-typed values. The extra section renders as 'extra_args=[str:token1, str:token2, ...]'. When requesting show=['values','extra'], the values section is printed first, then the extra_args line.

When underscores_to_dashes=True, option flags (those starting with '-') have their underscores replaced with dashes in the flag name on the command line, but the resolved attribute name keeps its underscore form. Positional arguments (registered without a leading dash) are never rewritten; their name stays exactly as declared.

Full typed-argument-parser surface: scalar conversions, collection/list conversions, booleans and flags, config precedence, known-only parsing, dash handling, from_dict, parse-twice lifecycle errors, post-init add_argument errors, subcommands/subparsers, accumulating actions, positional arguments, overriding inferred settings, and show/introspection modes.
Route by the supplied parser scenario fields. Public_test_cases define exact argv/configure/from_dict shapes, parsed values, usage-error categories, lifecycle errors, and show output.

The embedded public_test_cases are part of oracle_data and are authoritative for exact input shapes, action/operation names, stdout line order, whitespace, error categories, and deterministic formatting. When prose and expected_output differ, expected_output is the pass-critical contract.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: follow the same conventions as the existing error-reporting module so the calling system can process results uniformly
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The error output format mirrors the neutral error contract defined in the execution adapter (rcb_tests/dispatcher/run_case.py): one of error=usage, error=file_not_found, error=invalid_type_configuration, or error=invalid_request, each terminated by a newline.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same deterministic style we already use for set output in our metrics pipeline
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Set elements are rendered sorted lexicographically by token string (sorted(render_value(x) for x in v)), producing a '{t0, t1, ...}' format, as implemented in the render_value function in rcb_tests/dispatcher/run_case.py.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That question does not match a documented constraint. Please refer to the execution adapter contract in rcb_tests/dispatcher/run_case.py and the start.md specification for authoritative details.

feature01_typed_scalar.json.description: Declare a single argument annotated with a scalar type and an inline default value. When the command line does not supply the flag, the parsed value equals the declared default. When the flag is supplied, the parsed value is the string token taken from the command line. The rendered output lists the field name and its typed value.

feature02_untyped_argument.json.description: Arguments may be declared with only a default value and no type annotation, or added at configuration time with no annotation at all. An untyped argument keeps its default when the flag is absent; when supplied on the command line its value is taken verbatim as text (no numeric conversion is inferred). A configuration-time flag with no backing default simply captures the supplied text.

feature03_string_annotation.json.description: Type annotations may be provided as strings (forward references) rather than as live type objects; they are resolved to the same behavior. A scalar string annotation drives numeric conversion and a parameterized collection string annotation drives element-wise conversion of a whitespace-separated list. Defaults apply when flags are absent.

feature04_required_arguments.json.description: An annotated argument with no default value is required. If any required argument is missing from the command line the parse fails with a usage error. When every required argument is supplied the parse succeeds and the values are converted according to their annotations.

feature05_implicit_bool.json.description: A boolean argument with a default value becomes a no-argument flag whose presence flips it to the opposite of its default: a field defaulting to true reads as false when the flag is given, and a field defaulting to false reads as true when the flag is given. When neither flag is present both keep their declared defaults.

feature06_explicit_bool.json.description: In explicit-boolean mode a boolean argument expects a literal truth value as its argument. Truthy spellings (full word, single letter, or one) parse to true and falsy spellings (full word, single letter, or zero) parse to false, in any capitalization. Omitting the value after the flag, or omitting a required boolean entirely, is a usage error.

feature07_literal_choices.json.description: A literal-typed argument restricts the accepted values to a fixed set of allowed options and converts the chosen value to the type of the matching option (so a string option stays text, a numeric option becomes a number, and a boolean option becomes a boolean). Supplying a value outside the allowed set is a usage error. The default is used when the flag is absent.

feature08_optional_types.json.description: An optional-typed argument defaults to an empty (none) value and, when supplied, converts the command-line text to the inner type. Several optional arguments of different inner types can coexist; each is converted independently.

feature09_list_arguments.json.description: A list-typed argument consumes all whitespace-separated tokens following its flag and converts each element to the declared element type (bare list keeps strings, and typed lists convert to integers, floats, or booleans). Element order is preserved.

cases.expected_output:
list_=[str:I, str:was, str:wondering]
list_bool=[bool:True, bool:False]
list_int=[int:0, int:1, int:2]
list_str=[str:a, str:bee, str:cd, str:ee]

feature10_set_arguments.json.description: A set-typed argument consumes the whitespace-separated tokens after its flag, converts each to the declared element type, and collapses duplicate values into a unique collection (membership only, order not significant).

cases.expected_output:
set_int={int:1, int:2, int:3}
set_str={str:a, str:bee, str:cd}

feature11_tuple_arguments.json.description: A tuple-typed argument fixes the number and per-position types of its elements. A bare tuple keeps an arbitrary number of string elements; a single-element typed tuple yields one converted element; a homogeneous ellipsis tuple converts an arbitrary number of elements to one type; and a fully enumerated tuple converts each position to its own declared type.

cases.expected_output:
tup_bool=(bool:False, bool:True, bool:False, bool:True, bool:True)
tup_int=(int:2, int:3, int:4)

feature12_tuple_errors.json.description: A typed tuple enforces both the count and the per-position types of its elements. Supplying an element that cannot be converted to its declared type, supplying the wrong number of elements, or supplying elements in an order that violates the position types, all fail with a usage error. Declaring an empty tuple type is rejected up front as an invalid type configuration.

feature13_noncollection_default.json.description: If a collection-typed argument is given a default that is not itself a collection (for example an empty/none value or a bare scalar), and the flag is not supplied on the command line, the declared default value is returned unchanged without any collection conversion.

feature14_path_types.json.description: A filesystem-path type is supported as a scalar element type and inside optional, list, set, and tuple containers. Each supplied token is wrapped into a path value; collections of paths preserve their respective semantics (list ordered, set unique, tuple fixed-length).

feature15_union_requires_converter.json.description: A general union of two or more concrete types (other than an optional, i.e. a type combined with none) cannot be parsed automatically because the parser cannot know which member type to convert to. Declaring such a union without supplying an explicit conversion function is rejected as an invalid type configuration.

feature16_as_dict.json.description: After parsing, the full set of resolved arguments can be retrieved as a name-to-value mapping. The mapping includes typed fields, untyped fields, optional fields (with their none defaults), and collection fields, as well as arguments introduced under a renamed destination at configuration time.

cases.expected_output:
a=str:hi
b=int:1
c=bool:True
d=(str:hi, str:bob)
e=none
f={int:1}

feature17_from_dict.json.description: A parser can be populated directly from a name-to-value mapping instead of from the command line, reproducing the same retrievable arguments. If the mapping omits a required argument that has no default, population fails with an invalid-request error.

cases.expected_output:
a=str:hi
b=int:1
c=bool:True
d=(str:a, str:b)
e=int:7
f={int:1}

feature18_known_only.json.description: In known-only mode the parser consumes the arguments it recognizes and collects every unrecognized command-line token, in order, into a separate extra-arguments list rather than failing. Recognized arguments are still converted normally.

feature19_underscores_to_dashes.json.description: When underscore-to-dash conversion is enabled, multi-word flag names are spelled with dashes on the command line instead of underscores, while the resolved attribute names keep their underscore form. This applies to annotated fields and to flags registered at configuration time.

feature20_config_files.json.description: Arguments can be loaded from one or more configuration files, each containing command-line-style flag tokens. Command-line arguments override config values; among multiple config files, later files override earlier ones. Config content supports shell-style comments and quoted multi-word values.

feature21_config_errors.json.description: Configuration-file handling fails cleanly: pointing at a path that does not exist raises a file-not-found error; content that cannot be parsed as valid flag tokens raises a usage error; and a required argument that is still not provided by any config file or the command line raises a usage error.

feature22_actions.json.description: Configuration-time arguments can opt into accumulation behaviors: an append action collects one converted value per occurrence onto the list default; an append-constant action appends a fixed constant per occurrence; a count action tallies the number of occurrences onto a numeric default; and an extend action flattens multiple multi-valued occurrences into the default list.

feature23_subparsers.json.description: A parser can register named subcommands, each with its own arguments. Selecting a subcommand on the command line activates only that subcommand's arguments; the top-level arguments remain available before the subcommand token. When no subcommand is selected, only the top-level arguments are present. A subcommand's own arguments are unavailable unless that subcommand is chosen.

feature24_override_inferred.json.description: Re-declaring an annotated field at configuration time lets the developer override the inferred settings: a supplied default replaces the inferred one, and a supplied conversion type replaces the inferred type (so a field annotated as numeric can be forced to stay text). Flags may use a single dash, double dash, or both as aliases.

feature25_lifecycle_errors.json.description: The parser enforces its usage lifecycle. Parsing the command line more than once on the same parser instance is an invalid request, and adding a new argument after the parser has finished initializing is likewise an invalid request.

feature26_positional.json.description: An argument can be registered as a positional (no leading dash) at configuration time while still drawing its type from the matching annotated field, so the positional token is converted to the declared type. Positional names are never rewritten by underscore-to-dash conversion.
